refined_fr.refined_fr: Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model.
refined_fr.rationale: The candidate FR bundles three distinct obligations: (1) terrain analysis, (2) obstacle identification, and (3) work-zone representation. Each is a separate functional concern with different data sources, processing logic, and stakeholder consumption patterns. The refined FR is solution-neutral (avoids 'mapping/modeling' terminology which implies a specific DP) and focuses on the core problem: making spatial features available in a structured, queryable form. Splitting into atomic sub-FRs allows independent verification and design of each concern.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Confirm that a worksite model exists in a defined schema/format, contains spatial coordinates and feature classifications, and is accessible to downstream planning and execution functions per acceptance criteria.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-006.1: Acquire terrain elevation and surface properties across the worksite.
FR-NGHE-006.2: Identify and locate static obstacles (structures, vegetation, utilities) within the worksite boundary.
FR-NGHE-006.3: Define and represent active work zones and their operational constraints.
FR-NGHE-006.4: Integrate captured spatial features into a unified worksite model accessible to planning and execution functions.
refined_fr.note: The candidate FR is not atomic; it decomposes into four atomic sub-FRs, each addressing a single functional obligation. The refined FR statement serves as the parent requirement; sub-FRs should each be refined further and mapped to DPs independently.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire spatial feature data via multi-modal sensing (vision, range, inertial) and fuse into a unified coordinate frame; store as a structured spatial graph with feature attributes (geometry, classification, safety zone) queryable by planning/execution functions.

DP2: Decompose worksite into discrete zones; assign feature inventory per zone via manual or semi-automated annotation; represent as a hierarchical zone-feature registry with versioning and access control tied to operational policies.

DP3: Establish a real-time spatial streaming pipeline that ingests raw sensor observations, applies feature detection and classification filters, and publishes incremental updates to a shared spatial model accessible via standardized query/subscription interfaces.

DP4: Capture worksite features through a hybrid approach: static baseline model (from design/survey data) + dynamic overlay of detected obstacles/hazards; maintain separation between reference geometry and transient features to support policy-driven filtering.

DP5: Implement a feature abstraction layer that decouples raw sensor modality from logical feature representation; define a canonical feature schema (type, pose, confidence, safety attributes) and map all sensor inputs to this schema before storage/distribution.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP4 + DP5 (integrated): Establish a dual-layer spatial model comprising (1) a static reference layer derived from design/survey data representing persistent site geometry and safety zones, and (2) a dynamic detection layer capturing real-time obstacles and transient hazards. Decouple sensor modality via a canonical feature abstraction schema (type, pose, confidence, safety classification) that maps all inputs—vision, range, manual annotation—to a unified logical representation. Expose the composite model through query/subscription interfaces that allow planning and execution functions to filter by feature type, safety zone, and confidence threshold according to operational policies.

potential_sources: Tight coupling between sensor acquisition and feature representation if modality-specific formats are not abstracted early (mitigated by DP5 abstraction layer).

Coupling between spatial model and operational policy enforcement if safety filtering is embedded in model storage rather than at query/access layer (recommend policy-agnostic storage with policy-driven views).
Coupling between static baseline and dynamic updates if versioning/synchronization is not explicit (recommend clear separation with timestamped layers).
coupling_check.notes: DP4+DP5 achieves loose coupling by: (a) abstracting sensor modality behind a canonical schema, (b) separating reference geometry from transient features, (c) deferring policy-specific filtering to query time. Risk: if baseline model is stale or incomplete, dynamic layer may miss context; mitigated by explicit versioning and confidence metadata.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: A baseline worksite model (design/survey data) is available or can be rapidly established; Risk: if baseline is absent or severely outdated, dynamic detection alone may lack spatial context.

Assumption: Feature detection and classification algorithms achieve sufficient accuracy/confidence for planning/execution to trust the model; Risk: false negatives (missed hazards) or false positives (spurious obstacles) can degrade safety and efficiency.

Assumption: Operational policies are well-defined and stable enough to be encoded in query/filtering logic; Risk: if policies are ad-hoc or frequently revised, the model may become misaligned with actual operational intent.

Assumption: Planning and execution functions can tolerate latency in model updates (near-real-time vs. real-time); Risk: if latency is unacceptable, streaming architecture may require higher computational/network investment.
Assumption: Multiple sensor modalities (vision, range, manual) can be reliably synchronized and fused; Risk: temporal/spatial misalignment can introduce artifacts in the composite model.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) required latency for model availability (e.g., <100ms for dynamic hazards, <1s for static features), (2) minimum confidence/accuracy thresholds for feature detection by type (e.g., obstacle detection >95%, zone boundary >99%), (3) scope of 'agreed set of site features' (enumerate feature types: obstacles, hazards, zones, landmarks, access points), and (4) policy-driven access model (e.g., which roles/functions can query which feature types). This will sharpen DP evaluation and IM packaging decisions (e.g., whether to co-locate policy enforcement with model or separate it into a policy service).

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Unified spatial model management comprising static reference geometry layer, dynamic detection layer, canonical feature abstraction schema, and query/subscription interfaces for safety-aware planning and execution.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Ingest sensor data (vision, range, manual annotation) from Vehicle and Teleoperation; stream real-time obstacle and hazard detections to the dynamic layer.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Publish safety zone violations, confidence-filtered hazard notifications, and feature-type-specific alerts to planning and execution consumers.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receive operational policy parameters (confidence thresholds, safety zone definitions, feature filters); respond to planning queries on static/dynamic model state.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: spatial model, hazard detection, safety classification, and policy-driven filtering are core safety domain responsibilities. Static reference layer (design/survey data) and dynamic detection layer (real-time obstacles) both belong in safety services. Canonical feature abstraction (type, pose, confidence, safety classification) is a safety-centric schema. Query/subscription interfaces naturally expose safety state to downstream consumers. Minor diffusion: Vehicle and Teleoperation supply raw sensor telemetry; planning/execution modules consume filtered queries—both are external consumers, not diffusion.

Poor fit: Vehicle is a platform/hardware abstraction, not a safety logic layer. Spatial model, hazard detection, and safety classification belong in a safety service, not vehicle control. Static reference geometry and dynamic detection would diffuse across planning, execution, and teleoperation. Canonical feature schema would require duplication or tight coupling to safety logic elsewhere.

Poor fit: Teleoperation is a human-in-the-loop interface, not a spatial model or safety logic layer. The dual-layer model, feature abstraction, and policy-driven filtering are orthogonal to remote operation concerns. Containment here would scatter safety logic across teleoperation UI and backend, creating high coupling and diffusion.

Weak fit: NGHE Solution is likely a top-level system integration or orchestration layer. While it may coordinate safety services, it should not own the spatial model, detection logic, or feature abstraction. Placing DP4+DP5 here would blur the boundary between orchestration and domain logic, causing diffusion into planning, execution, and safety subsystems.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services is the natural owner of a unified spatial model because (1) hazard detection and safety classification are core safety responsibilities, (2) the dual-layer architecture (static reference + dynamic detection) mirrors the separation of persistent site geometry from transient obstacles—both safety concerns, (3) the canonical feature abstraction schema is a safety-domain contract that decouples sensor modality, and (4) query/subscription interfaces allow planning and execution to consume safety state without owning the model logic. This containment minimizes diffusion: sensors feed in via Telemetry, policies and queries flow via Tasking, and alerts flow out via SafetyAlert.
containment_risks: Risk: If planning or execution modules require direct write access to the spatial model (e.g., to annotate obstacles discovered during execution), the query/subscription interface may become bidirectional, increasing coupling. Mitigation: Design SafetyAlert or Tasking to support feedback loops (e.g., execution reports detected obstacles; Safety Services updates dynamic layer).

Risk: Static reference layer (design/survey data) may be owned or maintained by a separate data management or GIS service. If so, Safety Services must clearly define the interface for ingesting and updating reference geometry. Mitigation: Establish a clear data source contract (e.g., via Tasking or a dedicated configuration interface).

Risk: Multiple sensor modalities (vision, range, manual annotation) may have different latency, update rates, and reliability profiles. The canonical feature abstraction must handle these gracefully without losing fidelity. Mitigation: Confidence and timestamp metadata in the feature schema allow downstream consumers to apply their own filtering policies.

Risk: Query/subscription interfaces may become a performance bottleneck if planning and execution poll frequently or subscribe to high-volume updates. Mitigation: Design subscription filters (by feature type, safety zone, confidence) to reduce message volume; consider caching and delta updates.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Acceptance criteria require that 'a worksite model/map is generated and available to planning/execution functions for an agreed set of site features.' The Safety Services IM boundary directly owns this responsibility: it ingests sensor telemetry (Telemetry interface), maintains dual-layer spatial state (static reference + dynamic detection), and exposes the model via query/subscription interfaces (Tasking interface). Verification is concrete: (1) confirm static reference layer is populated from design/survey data, (2) confirm dynamic detection layer updates in real-time from sensor streams, (3) confirm planning/execution can query and filter by feature type/safety zone/confidence, (4) confirm model state is consistent with operational policies. Traceability is clear: FR → DP (dual-layer + canonical schema + policy-driven filtering) → IM (Safety Services module with defined interfaces).
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Excellent. The allocation of DP4+DP5 to Safety Services is unambiguous and well-justified. The module boundary explicitly owns spatial model management, hazard detection, safety classification, and policy-driven filtering—all core to the DP. External interfaces are clearly defined: Telemetry (sensor input), SafetyAlert (hazard output), Tasking (policy/query bidirectional). The containment score of 0.92 reflects strong cohesion. Planning and execution modules are explicitly identified as external consumers (not owners), eliminating ambiguity about who is responsible for the model. The canonical feature abstraction schema is owned by Safety Services, not scattered across sensor drivers or planning logic.

diffusion_risks: Risk: Bidirectional write access. If planning or execution modules discover obstacles during task execution and attempt to directly update the dynamic detection layer (rather than reporting via SafetyAlert feedback), the query/subscription interface becomes a write channel, increasing coupling and diffusion. Mitigation: Enforce that execution modules report observations via a dedicated feedback interface (e.g., extend Tasking or SafetyAlert to support 'detected obstacle' messages); Safety Services owns the update logic.

Risk: Static reference layer data source. If design/survey data is maintained by a separate GIS or data management service, Safety Services must clearly define the ingestion contract. Ambiguity here could lead to diffusion: reference geometry logic scattered between Safety Services and the external data source. Mitigation: Establish a formal data source interface (e.g., 'ReferenceGeometrySource' via Tasking) with versioning and update semantics.

Risk: Sensor modality heterogeneity. Vision, range, and manual annotation have different latency, update rates, and reliability. If the canonical feature abstraction schema is not sufficiently expressive (e.g., missing confidence, timestamp, or modality metadata), downstream consumers may bypass the schema and access raw sensor data directly, diffusing detection logic. Mitigation: Ensure the canonical schema includes confidence, timestamp, modality tag, and update rate; document filtering policies for consumers.

Risk: Query/subscription performance. If planning and execution poll the spatial model at high frequency or subscribe to unfiltered updates, Safety Services becomes a bottleneck. Pressure to optimize may lead to caching or delta-update logic scattered across consumers, diffusing the model state. Mitigation: Design subscription filters (by feature type, safety zone, confidence threshold) to reduce message volume; document expected latency and update rates in the Tasking interface contract.

Risk: Policy parameter ownership. Operational policies (confidence thresholds, safety zone definitions, feature filters) are received via Tasking. If policy interpretation or application logic leaks into planning or execution modules, the canonical safety classification becomes diffused. Mitigation: Safety Services must own all policy application; expose only the filtered/classified results to consumers, not raw policy parameters.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment. Safety Services is the natural owner of DP4+DP5 because: (1) Hazard detection and safety classification are core safety domain responsibilities, not vehicle control, teleoperation UI, or orchestration. (2) The dual-layer architecture (static reference + dynamic detection) mirrors the separation of persistent site geometry from transient obstacles—both are safety concerns and belong together. (3) The canonical feature abstraction schema is a safety-domain contract that decouples sensor modality; it is not a vehicle driver, teleoperation concern, or orchestration artifact. (4) Query/subscription interfaces allow planning and execution to consume safety state without owning the model logic, maintaining clear separation of concerns. (5) The module containment score of 0.92 reflects strong cohesion; diffusion score of 0.08 is minimal and attributable only to external sensor and consumer interfaces, which is appropriate. Comparison to alternative containers (Vehicle, Teleoperation, NGHE Solution) confirms poor fit: Vehicle is a hardware abstraction (containment 0.35), Teleoperation is a UI layer (0.28), NGHE Solution is orchestration (0.4). Safety Services is the clear winner.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP4+DP5 directly and sufficiently addresses FR: 'Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model.' The DP specifies: (1) a dual-layer spatial model (static reference + dynamic detection) that captures both persistent site geometry and real-time obstacles, (2) a canonical feature abstraction schema (type, pose, confidence, safety classification) that unifies all sensor modalities into a machine-readable representation, and (3) query/subscription interfaces that expose the model to planning and execution functions. This is a complete solution: the model is machine-readable (canonical schema), captures spatial features (geometry, obstacles, hazards), and is available to downstream functions (query/subscription). The DP is logically sound and non-redundant. No alternative DP is required. Acceptance criteria ('worksite model/map is generated and available to planning/execution functions for an agreed set of site features') are directly satisfied by the dual-layer model and query interfaces.

recommended_revisions: Revision 1: Formalize the static reference layer data source contract. Define a 'ReferenceGeometrySource' interface (or extend Tasking) to specify how design/survey data is ingested, versioned, and updated. Include schema for site geometry (e.g., GeoJSON, CAD format), update frequency, and conflict resolution if multiple sources exist.

Revision 2: Extend the canonical feature abstraction schema documentation to explicitly include: (a) modality tag (vision, range, manual), (b) timestamp and update rate, (c) confidence score and its interpretation (e.g., 0–1 scale, thresholds for 'high confidence'), (d) safety classification (e.g., 'hazard', 'obstacle', 'safe zone'), and (e) feature type enumeration (e.g., 'person', 'vehicle', 'debris', 'boundary'). Publish this schema as a formal contract in the Tasking interface.

Revision 3: Define feedback loop semantics for execution-discovered obstacles. Specify whether execution modules report observations via SafetyAlert (as a special message type) or via a dedicated 'ObstacleReport' sub-interface in Tasking. Include latency expectations and conflict resolution if execution reports contradict dynamic detection.

Revision 4: Document subscription filter semantics and performance expectations. Specify supported filters (by feature type, safety zone, confidence threshold, update rate), expected message volume, and latency SLAs. Include guidance on caching and delta-update strategies to prevent bottlenecks.

Revision 5: Clarify policy parameter ownership and application. Specify that operational policies (confidence thresholds, safety zone definitions, feature filters) are received via Tasking but applied entirely within Safety Services; planning and execution receive only filtered/classified results, not raw policies. This prevents diffusion of policy logic.

Revision 6: Add a 'Model Consistency' acceptance criterion: 'The spatial model maintains consistency between static reference and dynamic detection layers; conflicts are logged and resolved according to operational policy.' This ensures the dual-layer design does not create ambiguity or race conditions.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence, and the Safety Services IM boundary is well-justified with strong containment (0.92) and minimal diffusion (0.08). However, the gate analysis identifies six critical revisions that must be incorporated before proceeding to sub-level decomposition. These revisions address: (1) static reference layer data source contract formalization, (2) canonical schema completeness and formal publication, (3) execution feedback loop semantics, (4) subscription performance and filtering, (5) policy parameter ownership clarity, and (6) model consistency acceptance criteria. Given the high safety criticality of this FR (worksite spatial model is foundational to hazard detection and planning), these revisions are not optional refinements—they are essential to prevent diffusion, ambiguity, and safety-critical failures downstream. Proceeding to sub-level decomposition without these revisions would risk propagating incomplete or ambiguous contracts into lower-level FRs, increasing rework and safety risk. The revisions are targeted, concrete, and do not require re-architecting the DP or IM; they strengthen the current design by closing interface and ownership gaps.

revise.why: The DP (dual-layer spatial model + canonical feature abstraction schema + query/subscription interfaces) is logically sound and correctly mapped to FR. However, the DP specification lacks sufficient detail on: (1) the static reference layer data source contract (ingestion, versioning, conflict resolution), (2) the canonical schema structure and formal publication (modality tags, confidence semantics, safety classification enumeration, feature type enumeration), (3) feedback loop semantics for execution-discovered obstacles (interface, latency, conflict resolution), (4) subscription filter semantics and performance expectations (supported filters, message volume, latency SLAs), and (5) policy parameter ownership and application (policies received via Tasking but applied entirely within Safety Services). These details are critical to prevent diffusion and ensure the DP is implementable without ambiguity. Revise the DP specification to include these six elements as formal sub-specifications or interface contracts. This will strengthen the DP without changing its core logic and will enable unambiguous mapping to IM and downstream sub-FRs.

assumptions: Static reference layer data is maintained by an external GIS or design/survey service; Safety Services ingests it via a formal contract (to be defined in Revision 1).

Execution modules discover obstacles during task execution and report them via a feedback interface (SafetyAlert or dedicated sub-interface); Safety Services owns the update logic and conflict resolution.

Operational policies (confidence thresholds, safety zone definitions, feature filters) are received via Tasking and applied entirely within Safety Services; planning and execution receive only filtered/classified results.
Subscription filters (by feature type, safety zone, confidence threshold, update rate) are sufficient to prevent bottlenecks and reduce message volume to acceptable levels.
The canonical feature abstraction schema is expressive enough to represent all sensor modalities (vision, range, manual annotation) without requiring downstream consumers to access raw sensor data.
Model consistency between static reference and dynamic detection layers is maintained by Safety Services; conflicts are logged and resolved according to operational policy.
open_risks: Risk: Static reference layer data source ambiguity. If the ingestion contract is not formalized, Safety Services may receive conflicting or stale geometry data, leading to inconsistency between the reference layer and actual site conditions. Mitigation: Formalize the ReferenceGeometrySource interface with versioning, update frequency, and conflict resolution semantics (Revision 1).

Risk: Canonical schema incompleteness. If the schema does not include modality tags, confidence scores, timestamps, or safety classification enumerations, downstream consumers may bypass the schema and access raw sensor data, diffusing detection logic. Mitigation: Publish a formal, complete schema as a Tasking interface contract (Revision 2).

Risk: Execution feedback loop ambiguity. If execution modules are unclear about how to report discovered obstacles (SafetyAlert vs. dedicated interface), they may attempt direct writes to the dynamic detection layer, increasing coupling and diffusion. Mitigation: Define feedback loop semantics and latency expectations (Revision 3).

Risk: Subscription performance bottleneck. If planning and execution poll or subscribe at high frequency without filters, Safety Services becomes a bottleneck, creating pressure to scatter caching or delta-update logic across consumers. Mitigation: Design and document subscription filters and performance expectations (Revision 4).

Risk: Policy logic diffusion. If operational policies are exposed as raw parameters to planning and execution, policy interpretation logic may leak into those modules, diffusing the canonical safety classification. Mitigation: Clarify that Safety Services owns all policy application; expose only filtered/classified results (Revision 5).

Risk: Model consistency race conditions. If static reference and dynamic detection layers are updated asynchronously without explicit consistency semantics, planning and execution may observe stale or conflicting state. Mitigation: Add a 'Model Consistency' acceptance criterion and define conflict resolution (Revision 6).
evidence_needed: Formal specification of the ReferenceGeometrySource interface: schema format (GeoJSON, CAD, etc.), update frequency, versioning strategy, and conflict resolution rules.

Published canonical feature abstraction schema with: (a) modality tag enumeration (vision, range, manual), (b) timestamp and update rate fields, (c) confidence score definition and thresholds, (d) safety classification enumeration (hazard, obstacle, safe zone, etc.), (e) feature type enumeration (person, vehicle, debris, boundary, etc.), and (f) example instances for each modality.

Execution feedback loop specification: interface name and location (SafetyAlert extension or dedicated sub-interface), message schema for obstacle reports, latency expectations, and conflict resolution rules if execution reports contradict dynamic detection.

Subscription filter specification: supported filter types (by feature type, safety zone, confidence threshold, update rate), expected message volume under typical conditions, latency SLAs, and guidance on caching/delta-update strategies.

Policy parameter ownership clarification: document that operational policies are received via Tasking, applied entirely within Safety Services, and that planning/execution receive only filtered/classified results (not raw policies).

Model consistency acceptance criterion and implementation plan: define consistency semantics (e.g., 'static reference and dynamic detection layers are eventually consistent within 100 ms'), conflict detection and logging, and resolution strategy (e.g., 'dynamic detection takes precedence for safety-critical decisions').
